coredata.dc:title: Nanostructured manganese oxide on frozen smoke: A new water-oxidizing composite
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Hydrogen Energy Publications, LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications, LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Najafpour, M.M.
Salimi, S.
Balaghi, S. Esmael
Hołyńska, M.
Tomo, T.
Sadr, Moayad Hossaini
Soltani, Behzad
Shen, J.-R.
Veziroglu, T.N.
Allakhverdiev, S.I.
coredata.dc:description: The water-oxidizing complex or oxygen-evolving complex in plants, algae and cyanobacteria is an Mn4CaO5 cluster catalysing light-induced water oxidation. Herein we report that nano-sized Mn oxide/carbon aerogel is an active and low-density catalyst toward water oxidation. The composite was synthesized by a simple, low-cost procedure with different ratio of carbon aerogel to Mn oxide and characterized by scanning electron microscopy, energy-dispersive spectroscopy, high resolution transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, electronic spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and atomic absorption spectroscopy. Then, the water-oxidizing activity of this composite was considered in the presence of cerium(IV) ammonium nitrate. The composites with a high ratio of Mn oxide to carbon aerogel are good Mn-based catalysts with turnover frequencies of ∼0.33 (mmol O2/(mol Mn·s)). In addition to the water-oxidizing activities of these composites under different conditions, their self-healing reaction in the presence of cerium(IV) ammonium nitrate was studied. We also compare the composite with graphene quantum dots/Mn oxide, which is not stable under these conditions. Using hydrogen to store sustainable energies is a promising strategy in the near future and our results show that nano-sized Mn oxide/carbon aerogel is a promising catalyst for water-splitting systems toward hydrogen evolution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Artificial photosynthesis
Carbon aerogel
Manganese oxide
Nanotechnology
Oxygen
Self-healing
Water oxidation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the Mn oxide/carbon aerogel composite

Japan Society for the Promotion of Science

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: MMN and SS are grateful to the Institute for Advanced Studies in Basic Sciences. TT and JRS was supported by Grant-in-Aids for Scientific Research from the Ministry of Education of Japan (Grant No: 26220801 ), and by a grant-in-aid for Specially Promoted Research (No. 24000018 ) from JSPS, MEXT, Japan, respectively. This work was also supported by the Russian Science Foundation (Grant No: 14-14-00039 to SIA). SEB, BS and MHS are grateful for the financial support from Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University . The authors thank Maryam Fathollahzadeh for measuring resistance for A21.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The relation between the amounts of Mn ions in composites and the amounts of KMnO4 in solution.

XRD diffractograms for carbon aerogel (black), A15 (blue), A14 (olive) and A12 (red) (a). XRD diffractograms for A12 calcined at 673 K (b) and A20 dried at 60 °C (c). Pink arrows in b show related peaks for K-birnessite (K0.5Mn2O4·1.5H2O) with layered structure. * in c shows related peaks for KHCO3. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Sem (a) and EDX-Mapping for K (b) and Mn (c) in A21.
SEM image of the carbon aerogel (a) and A21 (b). SEM images of calcined A10 at 100 (c) and 500 °C (d). SEM images of GQD@MnOx (e,f).
TEM images of A11 (a) and of A10 dried at 100 °C (b). TEM images of GQD@MnOx (c–e).
Oxygen evolution diagrams for the Mn oxide/carbon aerogel composites.

The UV–Vis spectrum of Ce(IV) (0.044 M, 3 mL) in the presence of Mn oxide/carbon aerogel (10 mg, A8). Three peaks in the 500–580 nm range are attributed to the MnO4 − ions. The MnO4 − ions are formed in the first seconds and then disappear after Ce(IV) consumption (black line is baseline) (a). The UV–Vis spectrum of Ce(IV) (0.33 M, 5 mL) in the presence of MnOx/GQD (100 mg) (b).

Self-healing in water oxidation catalysed by Mn oxides in the presence of Ce(IV). 1: Oxygen evolution was detected with an oxygen-metre. The origin of oxygen is water. 2: Mn(II) was detected by EPR [52]. 3: the MnO4 − ions formation could be detected by UV–Vis in a reaction of Mn(II) and Ce(IV). 4: It is known that in the reaction of Mn(II) and MnO4 - at different pH values Mn oxide is produced. 5: The MnO4 − ions in the presence of Mn oxide oxidize water. In this reaction, the MnO4 - ions are reduced to Mn oxide. 6: Mn(II) in the presence of Ce(IV) forms Mn oxide. In a typical experiment, the reaction of MnSO4 in the presence of Ce(IV) (1.0 M) yields MnO2 that can be detected by XRD. Images and captions are taken Ref. [52]. Reprinted with permission from Ref. [52]. Copyright (2013) by Royal Society of Chemistry.
The effects of different parameters of the composites in water oxidation.
The rate of water oxidation catalysed by various Mn-based catalysts for water oxidation in the presence of a non-oxygen transfer oxidant.

head.ce:title: Nanostructured manganese oxide on frozen smoke: A new water-oxidizing composite

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The water-oxidizing complex or oxygen-evolving complex in plants, algae and cyanobacteria is an Mn4CaO5 cluster catalysing light-induced water oxidation. Herein we report that nano-sized Mn oxide/carbon aerogel is an active and low-density catalyst toward water oxidation. The composite was synthesized by a simple, low-cost procedure with different ratio of carbon aerogel to Mn oxide and characterized by scanning electron microscopy, energy-dispersive spectroscopy, high resolution transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, electronic spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and atomic absorption spectroscopy. Then, the water-oxidizing activity of this composite was considered in the presence of cerium(IV) ammonium nitrate. The composites with a high ratio of Mn oxide to carbon aerogel are good Mn-based catalysts with turnover frequencies of ∼0.33 (mmol O2/(mol Mn·s)). In addition to the water-oxidizing activities of these composites under different conditions, their self-healing reaction in the presence of cerium(IV) ammonium nitrate was studied. We also compare the composite with graphene quantum dots/Mn oxide, which is not stable under these conditions. Using hydrogen to store sustainable energies is a promising strategy in the near future and our results show that nano-sized Mn oxide/carbon aerogel is a promising catalyst for water-splitting systems toward hydrogen evolution.
Herein we report that nano-sized Mn oxide/carbon aerogel is a low-cost, low density, environmentally friendly and good catalyst toward water oxidation.

ce:section.ce:para: Nature uses sunlight to oxidize water and to reduce carbon dioxide. Inspired by Nature, artificial photosynthesis can be designed to capture light, oxidize water, and reduce protons or organic compounds to generate useful chemical fuels [1,2]. Hydrogen is a promising chemical to store sustainable energies in near future. Thus, in artificial photosynthetic systems, water oxidation is a key and a bottleneck reaction to generate cheap electrons [1,2]. Such cheap electrons can be used to reduce different compounds such as CO2 and water. The finding of an efficient, cheap and environmentally friendly water-oxidizing compounds is highly desirable [3–6]. Among different compounds [3–6], Mn oxides are very interesting [7–31] because they are not only cheap and environmentally friendly but also an Mn oxido cluster is efficiently used by Nature for water oxidation [32–39]. In other words, the water-oxidizing complex of Photosystem II is an Mn–Ca oxido cluster catalysing the light-induced water oxidation [32–39].

The water oxidation process catalysed by MnO2 in the presence of cerium(IV) ammonium nitrate (Ce(IV)) as an oxidant was reported in 1968 [40]. Since 1968, many strategies were used to find an efficient and stable Mn oxide-based water-oxidizing catalyst by many research groups [7–31].

Among different strategies, Mn oxide on carbon nanotubes [41,42], graphene [42], graphene oxide [42], C60 [43] or nanodiamonds [44] was introduced as a good catalyst toward water oxidation. In addition to water oxidation, applications of these nano-compounds in environmental chemistry, catalysis, energy conversion, and electrochemistry have been reported [45]. Combination of carbon nanostructures with Mn oxides improves their response time, efficiency, conductivity, and sensitivity. Strasser and Behrens reported on incipient wetness impregnation and a novel preparation method of MnOx/carbon nanotubes electrocatalysts for efficient water splitting [41]. The MnOx/carbon nanotubes sample obtained by conventional impregnation was identified as a promising catalytic anode material for water electrolysis at neutral pH showing a high activity and stability [41]. The water-oxidizing activities of nanostructured Mn oxides with C60, carbon nanotubes, graphene, graphene oxide, and nanodiamonds were also considered in the presence of Ce(IV) [42]. These composites are catalysts for water oxidation and, depending on the preparation method, show turnover frequencies of 0.2–2.6 mmol O2/(mol Mn·s) [42–45]. However, C60, carbon nanotubes, graphene, graphene oxide and nanodiamonds are expensive and some of them are not very stable under oxidizing conditions.

On the other hand, carbon aerogel is nanostructured carbon obtained from the carbonization of organic aerogels, which are prepared from the sol–gel polycondensation of certain organic monomers [46–48]. The product is a solid with low density and low thermal conductivity known as “frozen smoke”, “solid smoke” or “solid air” [46–48]. Basically, carbon aerogels have a network structure of interconnected nano-sized primary particles [46–48]. Coatings of nano-sized Mn oxide on a carbon nanostructure, where most of the active sites are at the surface, are promising. Such sites can do their work directly as water-oxidizing places and an interconnected 3D network structure that ensures fast electronic and ionic conduction through the electrode are the reasons to synthesize such composites [46,47]. Using a simple method, we report on nano-sized Mn oxide/carbon aerogel composite as a good water-oxidizing catalyst. In this procedure, the carbon substrate serves as a sacrificial reductant and converts aqueous MnO4 − to insoluble Mn oxide. This composite shows both water-oxidizing activity and self-healing reaction under water-oxidation conditions. Thus, it is promising as a water-oxidizing catalyst in water-splitting systems.

Carbon aerogel, reagents and solvents were purchased from the commercial sources and were used without further purification. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were taken using REM JEOL JSM-7500F and JEM-3010 UHR (JEOL Ltd., Japan, equipped with a retractable high-resolution slow scan CCD-Camera with GOS phosphorous scintillator and lanthanum hexaboride cathode as the electron source) devices, respectively. Mn atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) was performed on an Atomic Absorption Spectrometer Varian Spectrometer AA 110. Prior to the analysis, the composites were added to 1 mL of concentrated nitric acid and H2O2, left at room temperature for at least 1 h to ensure that the oxides were completely dissolved. The solutions were then diluted and analysed by AAS.
GQD were synthesized by the previously reported method [48].

100 mg graphene quantum dots (GQD) were added to 100 ml water and the solution was titrated by solution of KOH until the pH value changed to 7.5. In the next step, the solution was sonicated for 1 h and, subsequently, 0.09 g KMnO4 in 90 ml water was added to the solution. The colour of the solution rapidly changed from pale brown to colourless and the MnOx/GQD composite was formed. The solution was stirred for 4 days and the obtained solid was separated, washed carefully with distilled water to remove KMnO4 and dried at 60 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the Mn oxide/carbon aerogel composite
ce:section.ce:para: 100 mg of carbon aerogel was added to a solution of KMnO4 (details are in Table 1 ) and stirred for 24 h. The obtained solid was separated, washed carefully to remove KMnO4 and dried at 60 °C. Then, the solid was calcined at the reported temperature (details are provided in Table 1).

Water oxidation experiments in the presence of Ce(IV) were performed using an HQ40d portable dissolved oxygen-metre connected to an oxygen monitor with digital readout at 25 °C. In a typical run, the instrument readout was calibrated against air-saturated distilled water stirred continuously with a magnetic stirrer in an air-tight reactor. After ensuring a constant baseline reading, water in the reactor was replaced with a Ce(IV) solution. Without the catalyst, Ce(IV) was stable under these conditions and oxygen evolution was not observed. After deaeration of the Ce(IV) solution with argon, Mn oxides as several small particles were added, and oxygen evolution was recorded with the oxygen metre under stirring (Scheme S1). The formation of oxygen was followed and the oxygen formation rates per Mn site were obtained from linear fits of the data by the initial rate.

The Mn oxide/carbon aerogel composite was synthesized by a simple method:
(1) 4MnO4 − (aq) + 3C(s) + H2O(l) → 4MnO2(s) + CO3 2−(aq) + 2HCO3 − (aq)

Such catalysts [49] have advantages of both Mn oxides as good catalysts for oxidation reactions and of the carbon nanostructures as relatively good conductors. For example, resistivity as an estimation of the crust resistance of 105 Ω was reported for micrometre-thick layer of birnessite MnO2 [49], but resistance for A21 (Table 1) is 193 Ω.

XRD diffractogram for carbon aerogel is shown in Fig. 2 and Figs. S1–S4. XRD patterns for samples with low amounts of Mn oxides show no related lines for Mn oxides, but samples with enough Mn oxides such as A12 and A14 (Table 1) show lines at 2θ = 37 and 65° attributable to (100) and (110) for layered Mn oxides (Fig. 2). In the calcined A12 at 673 K these lines indicate the presence of K-birnessite (K0.5Mn2O4·1.5H2O) with layered structure (Fig. 2). However, under these conditions carbon aerogel is decomposed. At high pH values, XRD studies indicate the presence of KHCO3 which means that carbon aerogel is not stable under these conditions and decomposes to CO3 2− (Fig. 2). At low pH value more amorphous Mn oxide is formed which is a good catalyst toward water oxidation (Fig. 2).
EDX-SEM studies show a good dispersion of Mn oxide on carbon aerogel under these conditions (Fig. 3 ).

SEM images of the carbon aerogel and Mn oxide/carbon aerogel are similar (Fig. 4 ). However, as shown in Fig. 4, calcination at high temperatures decomposes the carbon aerogel and significantly changes the morphology of these composites.

The TEM images (Fig. 5 and Fig. S5) of these composites show layered Mn oxides (black section) with the distance of 7–8 Å between the layers. These Mn oxides are more crystalline than the ones prepared in the reaction of Mn(II) and KMnO4 in the absence of carbon. The HRTEM images show no special patterns for the MnOx/GQD composite (Fig. 5).

Compared with other supports, such as carbon nanotubes, graphene, graphene oxide, C60 or nanodiamonds:
1)
Carbon aerogel is more stable than graphene, graphene oxide or C60 [43] in the presence of KMnO4. Layered Mn oxides can form on carbon aerogel easily. Layered Mn oxides on graphene, graphene oxide and C60 [43] are not very stable and are reduced to MnO(OH) or Mn3O4.
2)
Layered Mn oxide on the carbon aerogel is more crystalline than the layered Mn oxide on many supports, such as carbon nanotubes [42], graphene [42], graphene oxide [42], C60 [43] which are prepared by similar methods, as detected by XRD and HRTEM.
3)
The amounts of Mn oxide which can be placed on the surface of carbon aerogel with our method are higher than the amount of Mn oxide placed on graphene, graphene oxide, C60 or even many metal oxides. This means high densities of the active sites for catalytic reactions.

FTIR spectra of the carbon aerogel show a broad band at ∼3200–3500 cm−1 related to antisymmetric and symmetric O–H stretching modes. A peak related to H–O–H bending at ∼1624 cm−1 is also observed. The absorption bands characteristic for an MnO6 core in the region ∼400–600 cm−1 assigned to the stretching vibrations of Mn–O bonds in the Mn oxide phase [50] were observed in the FTIR spectra of the Mn oxide/carbon aerogel composite (Figs. S6–S11).
The peaks in the 1200–1500 cm−1 range for composites prepared under alkaline conditions are assigned to carbonate ions. The ions are most probably formed in the decomposition of aerogel.
In the next step, we considered the water oxidation process catalysed by these composites.

We considered water-oxidation activity of the composite in the presence of Ce(IV). Ce(IV) is a non-oxo transfer agent, being a water-soluble, stable, strong and one-electron oxidant used to test water-oxidizing activity of various catalysts [3–6]. Ce(IV) oxidizes water-oxidizing catalysts and in the next step the oxidized catalyst oxidizes water. As indicated in Table 1 and in Fig. 6 , carbon aerogel without Mn oxide or with low amounts of Mn oxides shows no water oxidation in the presence of Ce(IV). This proves that very small amounts of Mn oxides on the surface of support are not stable and decompose to MnO4 − without water oxidation. Such effect was reported for very small amounts of Mn oxide on zeolite support [51]. The prepared composites at high pH value are also not efficient toward water oxidation and decomposition reactions occur under these conditions.
On the other hand, the prepared composites with a high ratio of Mn oxide at low pH are good Mn-based catalysts with turnover frequencies (TOF) of ∼0.33 (mmol O2/(mol Mn·s)) under these conditions.
The MnOx/GQD composite is significantly decomposed to KMnO4 in the presence of Ce(IV) and no oxygen evolution is observed under these conditions.

An important issue in design of catalysts is their stability. Among the different strategies to increase the stability of a catalyst, self-healing is very important. A few mechanisms for self-healing of Mn oxides under the water-oxidation conditions were reported by different groups. In the presence of Ce(IV), a self-healing reaction that remakes Mn oxide from MnO4 - was reported [52]. In this mechanism MnO4 − ions are produced as the product of the reaction of Mn oxide and Ce(IV) [52–55].

This is in accordance with the following observations (Scheme 1 ):
-
The MnO4 − ions are consumed by the end of the reaction when the concentration of Ce(IV) is decreased [52].
-
A solution of the MnO4 − ions in the presence of Mn oxide shows a linear decrease in concentration, provided that a reaction between MnO4 − and Mn oxide occurs [52].
-
The MnO4 − ions formation in the reaction of Mn(II) nitrate and Ce(IV) is observed (Scheme 1).
-
The amount of the MnO4 − ions in the absence of Mn oxide shows no change during 100 h of the experiment indicating that Mn oxide is necessary for the reduction of MnO4 −.
A mechanism was proposed for the self-healing of Mn oxide in the presence of Ce(IV).

As shown in Scheme 1, for self-healing an Mn oxide phase is necessary. As MnOx/GQD is completely decomposed to MnO4 −, reduction of MnO4 − to Mn oxide does not occur under these conditions. Although we could not observe self-healing under these conditions, it is possible that the products of the decomposition of GQD slowly react with MnO4 − ions to form Mn oxide.

It is important to note that the Mn oxide/carbon aerogel composites undergo a very similar self-healing process: Mn oxide/carbon aerogel immediately after the reaction with Ce(IV) forms MnO4 − ions (Fig. 7 ). The ions are not a catalyst for water oxidation in the presence of Ce(IV) (Fig. S12). These MnO4 - ions disappear completely after conversion of Ce(IV) to Ce(III). Our experiments showed that the MnO4 − ions react not only with Mn oxide/carbon aerogel composite, but also with carbon aerogel to remake the catalyst. To follow the reactions of the MnO4 − ions with Mn oxide/carbon aerogel or carbon aerogel, we used UV–Vis spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. S13, the concentration of a solution of MnO4 − ions decreases in the presence of Mn oxide/carbon aerogel or carbon aerogel.

To detect carbonate or other soluble carbon-based-compounds, we added Ce(IV) solution (1 mL, 0.5 M) to A10 (100 mg) under stirring and dried the resulting mixture. Then, D2O (0.5 mL) was added to the solid residue and the solution was examined with 13C NMR (Fig. S14). No soluble carbon based-compound was detected under these conditions.

As shown in Table 2 , Mn oxide/carbon aerogel are among good water-oxidizing compounds. Because both Mn oxide and carbon aerogel are stable, low-cost and environmentally friendly, the composites are promising for water-splitting systems at high scales. Carbon aerogel with low density can form low-density water-oxidizing catalyst. Such catalysts are easy to transport by e. g. spacecraft.

We synthesized nano-sized Mn oxide/carbon aerogel composite as water-oxidizing catalyst using a very simple method. The presented method is promising because the usage of KMnO4 provides high amounts of the nano-sized Mn oxide on carbon aerogel as stable, conductive, low-cost and environmentally friendly support. Such procedure for graphene, graphene oxide, C60 or nanodiamonds causes decomposition of these carbon nanostructures. The MnOx materials suffer from low conductivity and carbon aerogel can improve conductivity of the composite. Low amounts of Mn oxides on carbon aerogel show low activity toward water oxidation, but the composites with high amounts of Mn oxides are promising toward water oxidation with a TOF ∼0.3. On the other hand, the Mn oxide/carbon aerogel composite shows self-healing which is an important phenomenon stabilizing the catalyst. Carbon aerogel has low density and where a light water-oxidizing catalyst is needed, Mn oxide/carbon aerogel composite could find application. We also compared the composite with graphene quantum/Mn oxide, which is not stable under these conditions.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: MMN and SS are grateful to the Institute for Advanced Studies in Basic Sciences. TT and JRS was supported by Grant-in-Aids for Scientific Research from the Ministry of Education of Japan (Grant No: 26220801), and by a grant-in-aid for Specially Promoted Research (No. 24000018) from JSPS, MEXT, Japan, respectively. This work was also supported by the Russian Science Foundation (Grant No:14-14-00039 to SIA). SEB, BS and MHS are grateful for the financial support from Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University. The authors thank Maryam Fathollahzadeh for measuring resistance for A21.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2015.11.003.
